coredata.dc:title: A portable platform integrated with smartphones for rapid lactic acid analysis via ratiometric fluorescent imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

coredata.dc:description: This study presents a smartphone-integrated portable platform for rapid lactic acid (LA) analysis using ratiometric fluorescent imaging. The system employs a dual-emission probe composed of glutathione-modified gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) and carbon quantum dots (CDs), enabling visual detection via fluorescence color transitions under 365 nm UV excitation. As LA concentration increases, the fluorescence shifts from blue to orange. Smartphone photography and RGB color analysis reveal a nonlinear sigmoidal correlation between the red-to-blue intensity ratio (R/B) and LA concentration, modeled with a coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.998). The method exhibits high selectivity and achieves recovery rates of 94.5–104.8 % in sweat samples, validated against LC-MS. This technology offers a novel, on-site solution for food quality assessment and health monitoring applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Smartphone colorimetry
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing
Lactic acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Portable sensing platform for on-site LA monitoring using ratio fluorescence sensing.

Feasibility study of a nanomaterial-based sensor for lactate detection: fluorescence responses under different experimental conditions (Fe3+: 100.0 μM, LA: 50.0 μM) (Inset: corresponding fluorescence images under 365 nm UV Light: a: GSH-AuNCs + CDs; b: GSH-AuNCs + CDs + Fe3+; c: GSH-AuNCs + CDs + Fe3+ + LA).
(A) Fluorescence quenching behavior of GSH-AuNCs under different Fe3+ concentrations (λex = 365 nm); (B) quantitative relationship between τ0/τ ratio and Fe3+ concentration in GSH-AuNCs.
Morphological evolution of GSH-AuNCs and CDs upon addition of Fe3+ and LA: TEM Observations of (A) GSH-AuNCs Alone, (B) CDs Alone, (C) GSH-AuNCs with Fe3+, and (D) GSH-AuNCs with Fe3+ and LA.

Comprehensive spectroscopic characterization of GSH-AuNCs and CDs: (A) FT-IR Spectra of GSH (Black) and GSH-AuNCs (Red); (B) FT-IR Spectrum of CDs; (C) UV–Vis Absorption Profiles of GSH-AuNCs; and (D) UV–Vis Absorption Profile of CDs.
XPS survey spectrum of (A) GSH-AuNCs, (B) GSH-AuNCs in the Au 4f region, and (C) CDs.

Fluorescence characteristics of GSH-AuNCs and CDs: (A) Emission spectra of GSH-AuNCs at various excitation wavelengths; (B) emission spectra of CDs at various excitation wavelengths; (C) excitation-emission matrix of GSH-AuNCs; (D) excitation-emission matrix of CDs. Inset: Fluorescence Imaging Under 365 nm UV Irradiation.

Effect of pH and time on ratiometric probe fluorescence. (A) pH dependence of ratiometric fluorescence intensity in GSH-AuNCs + CDs; (B) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity changes in GSH-AuNCs + CDs; (C) pH effect on Fe3+-induced quenching; (D) Temporal analysis of Fe3+ quenching; (E) Dynamics of Fe3+ quenching; (F) Time-resolved recovery of LA.

Quantitative analysis of lactic acid detection using a ratiometric fluorescent probe: (A) concentration-dependent fluorescence behavior of the ratiometric probe (0–50 μM) with corresponding fluorescence imaging under 365 nm UV irradiation; (B) linear relationship between F600/F430 ratio and lactic acid concentration (0–50 μM) with linear fit analysis for the range of 2–35 μM.

Fluorescence chromaticity analysis of LA detection. (A) Relationship between R and B values and CLA (0–50.0 μM). (B) Color variations in the CIE 1931 (X, Y) diagram for different CLA (0–50 μM). (C) Scatterplot between the R/B ratio and LA concentration (0–50.0 μM).
Models for fitting experimental data. (A) the standard sigmoid model, (B) sigmoid model with amplitude, (C) sigmoid model with offset, and (D) sigmoid model with amplitude and offset.
Resistance of the GSH-AuNCs/CDs/Fe3+ ratiometric probe to interference from common analytes. (n = 5).
Fluorescence lifetime behavior of GSH-AuNCs in the presence of varying Fe3+ concentrations.

head.ce:title: A portable platform integrated with smartphones for rapid lactic acid analysis via ratiometric fluorescent imaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Traditional Chinese Medicine, Fuzhou First General Hospital Affiliated with Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, Fujian 350009, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Real-time lactic acid detection with GSH-AuNCs and carbon dots.

This graphical abstract presents a new method for detecting lactic acid (LA) using glutathione-protected gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) and carbon dots (CDs). The “off-on-off” fluorescence strategy involves Fe3+ quenching GSH-AuNCs (off), which is reversed (on) by LA chelation, changing the fluorescence color according to LA levels. This method allows real-time detection via smartphone-captured images under UV light, with RGB analysis for precise quantification. It offers a practical solution for monitoring lactic acid in food and drug safety and health diagnostics.

This study presents a smartphone-integrated portable platform for rapid lactic acid (LA) analysis using ratiometric fluorescent imaging. The system employs a dual-emission probe composed of glutathione-modified gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) and carbon quantum dots (CDs), enabling visual detection via fluorescence color transitions under 365 nm UV excitation. As LA concentration increases, the fluorescence shifts from blue to orange. Smartphone photography and RGB color analysis reveal a nonlinear sigmoidal correlation between the red-to-blue intensity ratio (R/B) and LA concentration, modeled with a coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.998). The method exhibits high selectivity and achieves recovery rates of 94.5–104.8 % in sweat samples, validated against LC-MS. This technology offers a novel, on-site solution for food quality assessment and health monitoring applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lactic acid (LA) has emerged as an essential monitoring parameter in sports medicine, with blood concentrations reflecting real-time metabolic states[1]. During anaerobic glycolysis, muscle glycogen breakdown generates LA as an energy byproduct[2]. Vigorous exercise induces characteristic concentration shifts: from baseline 0.5–1.5 mmol/L[3] to 12 mmol/L[4] during submaximal effort, reaching 25 mmol/L[5] in exhaustive conditions. Clinically, prolonged exposure to >5 mmol/L elevates acidosis probability, whereas >15 mmol/L disrupts myocyte calcium homeostasis. These thresholds justify integrating LA dynamics into personalized training algorithms and rehabilitation frameworks[6].

LA quantification technologies face inherent precision-portability tradeoffs. Gold-standard chromatographic methods (GC–MS/HPLC-MS) can achieve relatively low detection limits, but require a significant amount of money per analysis and a relatively long time for turnaround[7,8]. Electrochemical biosensors are more portable, yet they have issues with signal drift over time due to enzyme degradation[9,10]. These limitations have led to a renewed interest in fluorescence techniques. Fluorescence analysis has many advantages such as being simple, cost-effective, rapid, and highly sensitive. Fluorescence-based methods are increasingly regarded as promising tools for lactic acid detection[11–13].

The inherent limitation of single-emission systems manifests particularly in biological matrices, where autofluorescence interference can cause >20 % signal distortion. This fundamental challenge has driven ratiometric probe innovation through three technological leaps: Mao et al.[14] developed a fluorescence resonance sensor using CdZnTeS quantum dots functionalized with Rox-DNA. This sensor enabled the detection of fisetin and trypsin through a process likely involving some form of energy transfer mechanism, achieving a relatively low detection limit. Xiang et al.[15] fabricated a dual-emission sensor based on silica nanoparticles for nitrite detection. Their sensor achieved nitrite sensing through a surface plasmon-enhanced dual-emission process, with a certain difference in emission wavelengths, and showed a high recovery rate in serum samples. Yang et al.[16] proposed a ratiometric sensor for Cu2+ detection. This sensor utilized carbon dots that emit red light and polyethyleneimine-modified carbon dots that emit blue light. Charge transfer between these two types of dots enabled the detection of Cu2+ with a relatively high sensitivity in urine samples.

Ratiometric sensors offer remarkable advantages in terms of visualization. In comparison to monochromatic fluorescent sensors, they possess the ability to more readily distinguish intricate fluorescence color information of the target object[17,18]. Their dual-emission features enable a substantial wavelength separation, significantly enhancing the discrimination of the target against the autofluorescence background. A notable example is a ratio fluorescence sensor developed by Yang et al.[19] based on ZIF-8. This sensor displays a distinct color change, allowing for naked-eye detection. Despite these advancements, reports on the application of ratiometric fluorescent probes for LA detection are still scarce. Moreover, only a small proportion of the existing ratio probes can maintain a stable response under physiological pH conditions, and most of them encounter significant signal loss in high-chloride environments such as sweat.

This work presents the development of an innovative ratiometric sensor that makes use of glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) and carbon dots (CDs) for the monitoring of LA. As demonstrated in Fig. 1 , the fluorescence color transitions from blue to orange with increasing LA concentration. The resulting emission ratio shows a nonlinear sigmoidal correlation within a certain concentration range. This change can be captured by a smartphone-based color analysis method, enabling the visual detection of LA at a relatively low concentration.
The reagents used in the study can be found in the Supporting Information.
The Apparatus used in the study can be found in the Supporting Information.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized through a facile one-step hydrothermal strategy[20,21]. Ordinarily, 1.725 g/L of proline was added into and dissolved thoroughly in 20 mL of ultrapure water, generating a limpid and evenly distributed solution. Next, the obtained solution was then moved to a 50 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and then heated at 200 °C for a duration of 6 h. Upon cooling to the normal room temperature, the ensuing brownish solution was subjected to a purification process within a dialysis bag possessing a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 MWCO for 12 consecutive hours. In the end, the resultant solution was kept at 4 °C, ready for subsequent in-depth characterization and practical utilization.

GSH-AuNCs were produced by adapting a literature approach with some tweaks[22]. Before starting the experiment, every piece of glassware in use had to go through proper preparation. Initially, they were immersed in aqua regia, subsequently washed using ethanol, and finally subjected to ultrasonic cleaning with ultrapure water. To make the GSH-AuNCs, 10 mL of newly prepared 6 mM glutathione (GSH) was poured into 10 mL of 4 mM chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), and the two were stirred thoroughly. The resultant mixture was then stirred vigorously for 5 min under ambient room conditions. Afterward, it was heated up to 90 °C and maintained at that temperature for 6.5 h until the solution exhibited a pale yellow color. Eventually, the as-prepared GSH-AuNCs were placed in a refrigerator set at 4°C, reserved for subsequent utilization in further studies.
Detection of LA in Solution in the study can be found in the Supporting Information.

To assess the real-world practicality of the proposed method, we collected post-exercise sweat samples from athletes and diluted them 1000-fold. Using a smartphone camera, we captured fluorescence images under a 3 W, 365 nm UV lamp. Then, custom color-recognition software extracted the corresponding RGB values. To boost accuracy, we took multiple measurements for each sample and calculated the average R/B ratio. Furthermore, in order to prove the accuracy and reliability of the proposed method in testing actual samples, we tested the concentration of lactic acid in the same sweat samples by LC – MS (Liquid Chromatography – Mass Spectrometry) method.

Human sweat contains electrolytes (like Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl-, K+, and NH4 +) and metabolic products such as urea and glucose. To test the selectivity of our method, we added these potential interfering substances, one by one, to the GSH-AuNCs/CDs/Fe3+ mixture instead of LA, using the same procedure as before.

A Python-based data processing software was developed. It's for color recognition and quantitative analysis of fluorescence images. Fluorescence images captured by a smartphone can be analyzed by it. The read data can be saved as a text file. The concentration of LA can be determined using the relationship between the R/B ratio and the analyte concentration.

The software's operation process was recorded in a video tutorial. The video tutorial is provided as Supplementary material for this paper. The software can be downloaded by readers. They can follow the video tutorial to repeat the experimental procedures and data analysis.

The feasibility of the sensing system presented in this paper was evaluated by examining the fluorescence response under different conditions. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 , the ratiometric fluorescence probe, comprising GSH-AuNCs and CDs, displays two distinct fluorescence emission peaks at 600 nm and 430 nm under 280 nm excitation. The introduction of Fe3+ selectively quenches the fluorescence signal of GSH-AuNCs at 600 nm, signifying the formation of a GSH-AuNCs-Fe3+ complex. When LA is introduced, the fluorescence intensity at 600 nm gradually recovers, suggesting that LA can effectively displace Fe3+ from the GSH-AuNCs surface. Throughout the process, the fluorescence of carbon dots at 430 nm serves as a stable background blue signal. After the addition of iron ions, the fluorescence color first changes from red to blue, and then returns to red upon the addition of LA. These results demonstrate the feasibility of the sensing platform for detecting LA in complex biological samples.

The interaction between Fe3+ and GSH-AuNCs was examined to clarify the sensing mechanism. As previously reported[23], Fe3+ ions possess empty d orbitals, allowing them to interact with oxygen and nitrogen atoms located on the surface of AuNCs. These atoms can donate electrons to Fe3+, facilitating the charge transfer process and resulting in fluorescence quenching.

Extra GSH molecules within AuNCs can also interact with Fe3+, leading to cross-linking and fluorescence quenching. The electron transfer mechanism is consistent with fluorescence lifetime results. As shown in Table 1 , the fluorescence lifetime of blank GSH-AuNCs was 85.18 ns. With increasing Fe3+ concentration from 0 μM to 100 μM, the fluorescence lifetime decreased, suggesting dynamic quenching. This validates the electron transfer process in the GSH-Au/Fe3+ system. Fig. 3 illustrates how the fluorescence lifetime ratio (τ0/τ) changes with varying concentrations of Fe3+.

The Stern-Volmer equation adequately describes the experimental data, expressed as τ0/τ = 1 + KSV[Q], where [Q] represents the Fe3+ concentration and KSV denotes the quenching constant. The observed linear correlation between τ0/τ and Fe3+ concentration corroborates the occurrence of dynamic quenching.[24] Under 365 nm UV light, the hybrid fluorescent probe exhibits blue fluorescence due to the blue-green spectrum emitted by carbon quantum dots.

After LA was introduced into the system, the GSH-AuNCs/CDs sensing platform, modulated by Fe3+, showed increased sensitivity to LA due to fluorescence recovery. It is hypothesized that LA has a higher affinity for Fe3+ than GSH-AuNCs, displacing Fe3+ from the GSH-AuNCs surface. Additionally, LA can convert Fe3+ to Fe2+ through a redox reaction. The decrease in non-radiative electron transfer from GSH-AuNCs to Fe3+ leads to a restoration of fluorescence in GSH-AuNCs. As the concentration of LA increases, the fluorescence color transitions from blue to red, attributed to the red fluorescence emitted by the recovered gold nanoclusters.

The morphological features, particle size distribution, and dispersion characteristics of GSH-AuNCs and CDs were characterized via TEM analysis. Samples were placed on a carbon-coated copper grid. As depicted in Fig. 4 (A)–(D), spherical CDs and GSH-AuNCs show excellent dispersion. The particles are well-separated and do not form large aggregates. The average diameter of GSH-AuNCs was measured to be 1.36 ± 0.40 nm. CD particle sizes range from 2.5 nm to 4.0 nm. This size distribution ensures high surface area and reactivity. Upon adding Fe3+, GSH-AuNCs form a dense structure, likely due to interaction with Fe3+ ions. When LA is introduced, the dense structure reverts to a dispersed state. This suggests that LA can displace Fe3+ from GSH-AuNCs, restoring their original morphology and potentially enhancing functionality.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to verify the successful synthesis of GSH-AuNCs by comparing the spectra of GSH and GSH-AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the FT-IR spectrum of GSH-AuNCs does not display the absorption peak at 2521 cm−1, which is typically associated with the thiol group in GSH. This absence suggests the successful formation of Au-S bonds during synthesis, thereby confirming the encapsulation of gold nanoclusters by glutathione. Additionally, Fig. 5B displays the FT-IR spectrum of the blue CDs. The prominent peak around 3420 cm−1 corresponds to O–H/NH stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups on the CDs. The absorption bands at 2990 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of C–H bonds, respectively. The peak at 1630 cm−1 is assigned to C=O stretching vibrations, while the peaks near 1332 cm−1 suggest C-N stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of amide or imine groups in the CDs. As shown in Fig. 5C and 5D, the UV–vis spectra of GSH-AuNCs and CDs are presented. GSH-AuNCs show a faint shoulder peak near 400 nm, related to surface plasmon resonance. CDs show a broad absorption band from 250 to 290 nm, due to n-π* transitions. These results confirm the successful synthesis and optical properties of both GSH-AuNCs and CDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the bonding characteristics of the surface elements in the synthesized materials. As shown in Fig. 6 (A) and (B), the XPS analysis identified the presence of Au, S, C, N, and O elements. The Au 4f regional spectrum exhibited two distinct peaks: Au 4f7/2 and Au 4f5/2.[25] The observed peaks correspond to the Au(0) nucleus at 82.89 eV and 87.57 eV, as well as the Au(I) shell at 83.41 eV and 87.09 eV. These findings confirm the successful formation of Au-S bonds during the synthesis of GSH-AuNCs, which is vital for their stability and functionality. Additionally, the prominent peaks at 284.02 eV, 398.56 eV, and 531.56 eV correspond to the C1s, N1s, and O1s orbitals, respectively. These peaks indicate the presence of functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots (CDs), which are crucial for their optical and chemical properties. Overall, the XPS analysis aligns well with the expected chemical structures of GSH-AuNCs and CDs.

After preparing the ratiometric probe, experiments were conducted to optimize key parameters for fluorescence detection, including excitation wavelength, pH, and reaction time. Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra were analyzed to find the optimal excitation wavelength. For GSH-AuNCs, an excitation peak at 205 nm with emission at 600 nm showed decreasing intensity with higher excitation wavelengths. CDs exhibited an excitation peak at 226 nm and emission at 430 nm, also decreasing in intensity with increased excitation. Visual inspection indicated that the optimal excitation wavelength was 280 nm, balancing fluorescence intensity for both GSH-AuNCs and CDs for effective detection (see Fig. 7 ).

The following condition optimization experiments investigated the effects of pH, reaction time, and Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence response of the ratiometric probe. A 10 mM PBS buffer (pH 7.4) was used to control the pH. As shown in Fig. 8 , the fluorescence intensity ratio (F600/F430) of the probe remained nearly stable between pH 2.0 and 10.0. This indicates that the probe is highly tolerant to pH variations. The fluorescence intensity ratio was also evaluated over time. The probe maintained a stable ratio for up to 100 min. Considering maximum fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ and optimal fluorescence recovery after adding LA, pH 4 was determined to be the optimal condition for the determination of LA. Upon adding 100 μM Fe3+, maximum fluorescence quenching was achieved within one minute. After adding LA, the fluorescence of GSH-AuNCs recovered within eight minutes. The optimal reaction time for LA was determined to be 8 min. By optimizing the parameters (pH value, Fe3+ concentration, and reaction time), the ratiometric fluorescent probe achieves high reliability for LA detection in complex samples.

Under optimal detection conditions, different concentrations of LA were introduced into the ratiometric fluorescence probe consisting of GSH-AuNCs, CDs, and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 9 A, the peak of CDs at 430 nm remained constant, while the fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuNCs at 600 nm gradually recovered as LA concentration increased from 0 to 50 μM.

The inset shows that the fluorescence color changes from blue to red with increasing LA concentration. This color change is due to the interaction between LA and the GSH-AuNCs/Fe3+ system, which modulates the fluorescence properties. As shown in Fig. 9B, there is a strong correlation between the F600/F430 ratio and LA concentration, with a linear relationship between 2 and 35 μM. The linear equation is:
(1)
F600/F430 = 0.0149 CLA /(μM) + 0.238 R2 = 0.995
CLA is the LA concentration, and R is the correlation coefficient. The LOD was calculated using the formula LOD = 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation from three blank measurements, and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD value was found to be 0.67 μM.

To enhance the convenience of measuring LA concentration, a smartphone was used to capture the fluorescence images instead of a traditional fluorometer. The images were then analyzed using computer software to extract the RGB values. As shown in Fig. 10 A, using the red or blue channel signals alone for fitting of lactate concentration resulted in poor correlation. This may be due to different lighting or camera angles during image capture, affecting the comparability of image light intensity and signal concentration dependence. By evaluating the relationship between the R/B ratio and lactate concentration, it was found that there is a quantifiable non-linear relationship between them (Fig. 10). This indicates that using the R/B ratio can effectively reduce the impact of environmental factors on fluorescence intensity measurements, improving the accuracy and robustness of LA detection.

Fig. 9B's data collected using a fluorescence spectrometer showed good linearity within the valid concentration range, while subsequent mobile phone imaging data had poor linearity so non-linear fitting was used instead. For model selection in Fig. 10C, sigmoid models were chosen to describe the S-shaped relationship between lactate concentration (LA) and fluorescence ratio (R/B). Four models were compared using R2 values: Standard (0.9699, Fig. 11 A), Amplitude (0.9855, Fig. 11B), Offset (0.9909, Fig. 11C), and Amplitude + Offset (0.9982, Fig. 11D). The Amplitude + Offset model performed best because it considers both maximum fluorescence intensity and baseline shifts, resulting in the optimized formula R/B = 0.68/(1 + exp(−0.12×(CLA/(μM)-26.70))) + 0.28 (R2 = 0.998) (Equation 2) for quantifying LA between 0–50 μM.

Table S1 demonstrates the performance of conventional fluorescence spectrophotometry and other methods for the detection of lactic acid, and it can be seen that the proposed smartphone imaging method has a lower detection limit and higher sensitivity, proving its capability in lactic acid detection. This novel method for LA determination, contributes valuable insights to the field.

The “off-on-off” ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy used in this study employs GSH-AuNCs and CDs as fluorescent probes. The fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuNCs increases with higher LA concentrations, while CDs provide a stable background signal. When conducting quantitative analysis using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, ratiometric fluorescence does not offer enhanced accuracy compared to single-wavelength fluorescence. However, in outdoor settings, using a smartphone to capture fluorescence images under 365 nm UV light and processing the RGB values with a computer offers advantages. This method eliminates the need for large, sophisticated equipment and enables simultaneous imaging of batch samples, thereby significantly improving efficiency. Variations in light sources and camera angles can affect fluorescence readings. External factors may disrupt the stability of CDs' fluorescence and the response of GSH-AuNCs to LA concentrations. Therefore, correlating the R/B ratio with LA concentration using a ratiometric approach is crucial for accurate corrections and reliable on-site detection (see Fig. 12 ).

Selectivity and interference resistance are crucial for evaluating detection methods. To assess the anti-interference capability of our sensor for lactic acid (LA) detection, common interfering electrolytes (Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl-, K+, NH4 +) and metabolic byproducts (urea, glucose, ascorbic acid) were introduced. These substances are often found in biological samples and can interfere with detection.

As demonstrated in Fig. 11, the interfering substances had negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of the GSH-AuNCs/CDs/Fe3+ system. This highlights the sensor's excellent selectivity and robust anti-interference performance, rendering it ideal for detecting LA in complex biological environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of LA in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of LA in actual samples, the applicability of the established sensing strategy was assessed using the standard addition method to measure LA in sweat samples. As shown in Table 2 , when LA was added at concentrations of 0, 5.0, and 15.0 μM, the recovery rates ranged from 94.5 % to 104.8 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) between 2.62 % and 5.21 %. This indicates that the proposed method is highly reliable. When comparing the test results of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor with those of the LC-MS test, it was found that the results were basically consistent within the error range. Fig. S1 is the mass spectrum of the sweat sample, and the molecular ion peak of C3H5O3 − is at 89.02 and C3H4O3Na− is at 112.99. The experimental results demonstrate that this sensing strategy is highly suitable for the quantitative determination of LA in real samples.

This paper introduces a portable sensing platform for the sensitive, rapid, and visual quantification of LA. The platform uses ratiometric fluorescent imaging integrated with smartphone. It employs an “off–on–off” fluorescence strategy based on GSH-AuNCs and CDs. The system enables on-site detection in complex samples such as sweat and eliminates the need for large equipment. The platform has a nonlinear sigmoidal correlation between the red-to-blue intensity ratio (R/B) and the lactic acid concentration. It shows high selectivity and was successfully applied to real sweat samples. Recovery rates were from 94.5 % to 104.8 %. RSD was between 2.62 % and 5.21 %. This work provides a robust tool for monitoring LA levels. It has a wide range of applications and important roles in many scenarios, such as in wearable devices, real-time detection of lactic acid, which is crucial for sports medicine, clinical diagnostics and food safety. Future work will focus on optimizing the platform. Its applications in health diagnostics and environmental monitoring will be explored.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Scientific and Technological Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2023J01052).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126224.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Anal. Bioanal. Chem.

Biosens. Bioelectron.
